title: Data Analysis and Statistics for Political Science
text:
Preface

This is a book primarily for undergraduate students taking a course in data analysis and statistics. Many statistics and data science books are geared toward general audiences or statistics students. Those books typically do not contain many political science examples. This book is designed to gives students a foundation in data analysis and statistics but with explicitly political science examples. The use of examples helps solidify complicated concepts surrounding statistics. Political science students will relate to these political science examples and facilitate mastery of the content.
Book outline

title: About the Author
text:
Brandon L. Bartels is a Professor of Political Science at George Washington University. His research and teaching interests center on American politics, judicial politics, the U.S. Supreme Court, public perceptions of law and courts, institutional legitimacy, judicial power and court-curbing in comparative context, and political methodology. Brandon’s work has appeared in the American Political Science Review, American Journal of Political Science, Journal of Politics, Public Opinion Quarterly, and other outlets. His research has also been supported by the National Science Foundation. His book with Christopher D. Johnston, Curbing the Court: Why the Public Constrains Judicial Independence, was published in 2020 by Cambridge University Press.
Brandon has been teaching data analysis and statistics courses for over 20 years. He has taught undergraduate statistics as well as several Ph.D. courses. This book is a culmination of many of the things he has taught and learned over time – including from his students.

title: 1  Using R and RStudio
text:
1.1 Installing R and RStudio
R is an “open-source” statistical software package that is free of charge. You will download this program to your computer. To run R on your computer, you will also need to download and install a program called “RStudio.” As Ismay and Kim put it, think of R as the engine of your car and RStudio as your dashboard and other levers inside the car. You will use RStudio (the dashboard features, the steering wheel, and gas pedal) to run R (the engine). Once you have downloaded and installed R, you will never need open it again (just like you may have never opened the hood of your car to inspect the engine). Instead, you will perform all statistical computing commands using RStudio. Every once in a while (perhaps once per year), you will need to update your versions of R and RStudio on your computer. I will go over procedures for this below.
As a statistical program, RStudio runs on “packages.” As Ismay and Kim put it, think of RStudio as your smart phone and think of these packages as apps that you download and use. RStudio includes what are known as “base” packages that are available to use once you install R and RStudio. These include some basic commands for summarizing variables and doing regression analysis. Moreover, most of the commands a typical RStudio user will employ involve add-on packages that you need to install and load. Once you install a package, it stays on your computer However, every time you open RStudio (after closing it previously), you need to load packages. I will discuss this below.
This book will also discuss a program called Quarto, which is available to use in RStudio. Using this program, one can create .pdf and .html documents that integrate graphical and statistical results output with your narrative explaining your results—all in a single document. One can actually write an entire paper using Quarto that includes your writing and your analyses. Quarto renders this document into a .pdf or .hmtl. Some instructors have their students complete assignments for a class using Quarto.
Installing R and RStudio is easy and takes 5-10 minutes. Download and install R first using this link: https://cloud.r-project.org/.
Next, download and install RStudio via this link: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/. Skip step 1 (“Install R”), since you have already done that. Go to step 2 (“Install RStudio”) and click on your operating system. You can also scroll down to “All Installers and Tarballs.”

title: 1  Using R and RStudio
text:
1.2 RStudio as Your One-Stop Shop
As discussed, you will use RStudio to do all of your analyses. You never need to open R itself. RStudio can run R without R being opened. When you open RStudio for the first time, you will see something like this image:
